title: Current patterns and loss contributions in CORT cables carrying AC current
authors: Benoît Vanderheyden, Julien Dular, Louis Denis, Steffen Elschner, Christophe Geuzaine, Mariusz Wozniak, Francesco Grilli
abstract: Conductor-on-round-tube (CORT) cables are a potential solution for carrying AC power in a small cross-section. Due to the geometry of the cable and the helical arrangement of the coated conductors (CC), the current follows a non-trivial pattern inside each CC. For instance, for the case of a single-layer cable, the current flow is mostly axial along the outer face of the CCs and mostly azimuthal along their inner face. Such a current distribution, known as the Garber current pattern, affects the transport AC losses. In numerical models, commonly adopted simplifications are either based on straight conductors or infinitely thin CCs. Such approaches neglect the Garber current pattern and thus misrepresent both the detailed current flow within the CC and the resulting 3D distribution of the fields. In this work, the detailed 3D current distribution in the CCs is investigated in a one-layer CORT cable, as a function of the cable geometrical parameters such as the conductor thickness, the pitch angle, and the gap between adjacent CCs. In particular, the impact of the Garber current pattern is studied on the two largest contributions to the AC losses, namely the surface losses (associated with the penetration of the component of the magnetic field parallel to the wide faces of the superconducting layer) and the edge losses (associated with the penetration of the perpendicular component of the magnetic field occurring in the vicinity of the gaps between the CCs). The detailed distribution of the currents in the CCs is examined and its relationship with the different AC loss mechanisms is established. This study is carried out by means of an effective 2D model that uses a system of coordinates conforming with the helical structure of the cable.
summary: This paper analyzes AC losses in CORT cables by resolving the complex, three-dimensional current flow patterns within the coated conductors. Using an effective 2D helicoidal model, it demonstrates that simple analytical approximations fail to capture the true loss mechanisms, which depend critically on the cable's geometry and gap size.
main_problem: The study investigates the complex current patterns and resulting AC power losses within Conductor-on-Round-Tube (CORT) cables carrying alternating current.
main_result: The detailed 3D current distribution is shown to exhibit a 'Garber pattern' alongside edge effects, leading to AC losses that deviate significantly from simplified analytical models, especially at small gaps.
method: The research employs an effective 2D helicoidal model derived from first principles to simulate the non-trivial current flow in the superconducting coated conductors.
model_or_system: The physical system is a CORT cable composed of multiple coated conductors wound helically. The analysis models the AC losses by considering three main contributions: surface losses, edge losses, and diving current losses.
key_observables: Current density components (axial $J_z$, azimuthal $J_\phi$, radial $J_r$), total AC power loss ($Q/L$), and the relative dominance of different loss mechanisms.
important_parameters: Conductor thickness, gap size ($g$), pitch angle ($\alpha$), current amplitude ($I_0$), and the exponent $n$ in the resistivity power law $ho \propto J^n$.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a power-law relationship for resistivity and neglects complex field dependencies of critical current density on magnetic flux magnitude or direction.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spatial distribution of current densities ($\mathbf{J}$) components across different regions (Garber, Mawatari) within the cable cross-section, and plot calculated AC losses versus gap size and pitch angle, comparing simulation results to analytical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the geometry and resulting current flow patterns in CORT cables. It compares detailed 3D simulations using a helicoidal model against simplified theoretical models (Surface/Mawatari) to quantify how geometric parameters dictate which loss mechanism dominates.
why_interesting: While focused on superconducting magnet technology, the rigorous modeling of non-uniform current distributions and field penetration in confined geometries shares mathematical parallels with understanding flux dynamics or boundary effects in quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Application of Ampere’s law to a CORT cable with three CCs with a very small gap. (a) The magnetic field produced inside resembles that of a long solenoid. (b) The magnetic field produced outside resembles that of a long wire. (c) The resulting magnetic flux density is axial inside and azimuthal outside. Thus, the CCs carry an azimuthal current density on their inner face and an axial current density on their outer face.

Fig. 2. Geometry of the single-layer CORT cable and parameter definition. (a) 3D-view, where R is the mean radius, g the width of the gap between CCs, w the CC width, α the pitch angle, and P the pitch length. (b) Cross section at a fixed value of z. Only one half of a CC is shown and the CC is artificially enlarged for clarity. Here, d is the CC thickness, r and θ are cylindrical coordinates and β is half the angular size of the CC as seen from the z-axis in the x-y plane.

Fig. 3. Various definitions for representing the current distribution in the CC. Left: half-CC viewed along the cable axis from above. Paths along which the current density is represented: AB, along the inner edge, BC, along the gap edge, CD, along the outer edge, and DA, along the conductor symmetry line (see Fig. 2 (b)). Here, Jz, Jϕ, and Jr are respectively the axial, azimuthal, and radial components of the current density. Relative variables u ∈[0, 1] and v ∈[0, 1] are used, with (0, 0) representing the gap vertex on the inner CC edge and (1, 1) the intersection of the conductor symmetry line and the outer CC edge. Right: unrolled plot of two CCs, see Fig. 2 (a). Definition of unit...

Fig. 4. Current density in one half of a CC for the reference geometry with a gap of 100 µm and n = 25. The components of the current density are represented along the loop ABCDA as a function of the cumulative path index, which is normalized in such a way that it increases by one unit along each leg of the loop. The Garber region is colored in yellow and exhibits a current density that is axial near the outer face of the CC and azimuthal near the inner face of the CC. The Mawatari region is colored in lavender and exhibits a current density that flows along the edge unit vector ee, in this case with Jϕ = Jz (α = π/4). Fig. 5. Current density components along a radial path within the transition region (v = 0.015). Reference geometry with α = π/4, g = 100 µm, and n = 25.
